title: Dictionaries
beginnerExplanation: Key-value collections for named pieces of data. Unlike lists which use numerical indexes, dictionaries use descriptive keys to find values.
intermediateExplanation: To represent structured data. For a player, you want to store health, max_health, strength, and name. Dictionaries map these property names directly to their values.

realWorldAnalogy.title: A Phonebook
realWorldAnalogy.description: In a phonebook, you look up a person's name (the Key) to find their phone number (the Value). You don't look them up by their order number.

mentalModel: In a phonebook, you look up a person's name (the Key) to find their phone number (the Value). You don't look them up by their order number.

commonMistakes: Using a key that does not exist directly with brackets, causing a crash.

debuggingMission.scenario: An adventurer is trying to use Dictionaries but hit a common pitfall: Forgetting quotes around string keys.
debuggingMission.explanation: Make sure to follow the best practices: Use descriptive strings for keys.

practiceQuestions.question: How do you safely access a key that might not exist in a dictionary without causing an error?
practiceQuestions.explanation: dict.get(key) returns None (or a default value you specify) if the key doesn't exist, preventing a KeyError crash.

challengeQuestions.question: What is the correct keyword or operator most commonly associated with Dictionaries?

projectExercise.title: Mastering Dictionaries
projectExercise.description: Implement a clean snippet demonstrating the use of Dictionaries.
requirements: Write a snippet utilizing the syntax: my_dict = {"key": value, "key2": value2}
